dialogue.utterance: Unit 3 responding for breathing problems at [CITY], Apt #312.
Hello sir, I'm Alexa, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
Shortness of breath, it's been on since Thursday, got worse the past day or so.
Are you alert and responsive today sir? Would you say you're awake and understanding right now?
Yes, my name is Harold, and I'm in [CITY]. I'm feeling pretty short on breath though.
Good. Would you tell me what day it is?
It's January 21st. I need to move soon-I've been standing here too long.
Harold, can you speak for me? Can you take a deep breath?
Airway is okay. I'm breathing, just struggling to get through it. I can't seem to catch a full breath.
Your airway is patent and self-maintained. Stay in your current position for now while I continue checking you over.
Your breathing is fast, around 30 breaths a minute, with accessory muscle use. Are you wheezing or feeling pressure in your lungs?
It feels like there's a lot of fluid in my lungs. I can barely get a word out.
Thank you. Your oxygens were at 75 on room air. Let's give you O2 nebulizer treatment to help ease your breathing.
Radial pulse is strong and regular. Skin is normal color and warm and dry. Pulse is 68.
Blood pressure starts at 191/101.
Respiratory rate is 21, shallow and labored with audible wheezing.
We obtained a pulse of 68. No signs of tachycardia.
O2 saturation with nebulizer is 99%, which is a big improvement from initial readings.
4-lead EKG showed sinus bradycardia and occasional premature atrial contractions.
Let me confirm treatment and dosage for nebulizer medication.
Glucose measured as 102, which is in the normal range.
We are administering 5mg of albuterol and 0.5mg of atrovent via nebulizer now.
You mentioned having COPD and CHF in the past. Are you taking any medications for those conditions?
I'm on Bumex, as prescribed, and follow the rest of my regimen.
Have you noticed an increase in swelling in your legs lately?
Yes, the swelling has become worse in my lower legs.
Did anything in the past 24 hours contribute to your worsened breathing?
I saw my PCP this morning and was told I had fluid in my lungs.
On a scale of 0 to 10, how would you rate your shortness of breath now?
It's 10 for the past hour.
I see you have +3 pitting edema in your lower legs. Other skin findings are normal.
Yes, the pitting goes deeper than just soft tissue swelling-significant water retention.
Do you feel any stiffness in your neck or trouble visualizing straight ahead?
Any discharge from the nose or mouth?
I have no nasal or oral discharge to my knowledge.
I'm going to examine your chest and back for lung sounds. May I proceed?
Yes, go ahead.
I'm hearing decreased breath sounds, with some wheezing and crackles in the lower lobes.
We'll start you on CPAP for now with positive end-expiratory pressure (PEEP) at 5 cmH2O.
Per the CP and medical history, we'll follow both pulmonary edema and respiratory distress protocols.
Updated blood pressure is now 162/110.
Respiratory rate has increased to 28 breaths per minute.
Pulse rate is 69 and O2 stays at 99%. No significant change.
I'll adjust the CPAP to maintain PEEP at 5 cmH2O.
SpO2 slightly dropped to 98%, but that's within acceptable limits.
Harold, where would you prefer we transport you for care?
[COMPANY_NAME] if you think it's best.
We need to move you to the stretcher now. Keep your arms positioned as we assist you.
Harold is now secured on the stretcher with five-point restraints.
Continuous 4-lead monitoring shows sinus rhythm with PVCs. No evidence of STEMI.
No life-threatening arrhythmias observed at this time.
IV access established using an 18G at the left antecubital vein with saline lock.
We increased the CPAP PEEP up to 7.5 cmH2O due to continued shortness of breath.
Blood pressure now at 217/100, respiratory rate is 29, SpO2 is 98%.
Blood glucose remains stable and in the normal range, no concern there.
What medications do you currently take regularly?
I use Bumex, lisinopril, spironolactone, and others that support heart and lung function.
You're safely secured on the stretcher now, ready to be moved into the ambulance.
On a 0-10 scale, how severe is your shortness of breath now?
It's come down to about 7 out of 10 now. Feeling better.
12-lead EKG repeated en route with no STEMI or other findings of significance, consistent with initial results.
Harold, let me go ahead and secure you again with five-point restraints inside the ambulance.
All equipment and restraints are placed properly.
Let's begin moving you into the ambulance now. It will get a bit bumpy at first.
Hospital staff have been contacted and are aware of the en route arrival.
From a safety standpoint, we are fully set in.
You're now loaded and comfortably positioned within the ambulance for transport.
Holding the CPAP at 7.5 cmH2O and monitoring the mask seal and comfort.
I will now start the ambulance. Please remain stable until we give the signal.
You'll remain on CPAP-assisted ventilation. The nebulizer is still available in case needed.
Currently, the BP is elevated but not alarming in terms of trends.
Harold is securely fastened in the ambulance and stable.
Remain in place for the duration of transport, stay with us.
I confirmed your glucose again-it stays in the safe range.
Estimated time of arrival to [CITY] is about 15 minutes.
I do feel better compared to when I started, thank you.
On a scale of 1 to 10, would you say you're over a 2 now in terms of shortness of breath?
Yes, it's very much manageable now.
That's great progress. You're doing well.
We've got a smooth ride ahead as we make our way.
We'll notify hospital staff if there's any deterioration during the ride.
The IV remains functional in case of an emergent dose need.
Vital signs are still under continuous monitoring for SpO2, BP, and heart rhythms.
No pressure is on you-we'll handle the rest.
You sure I can't keep this CPAP?
We can definitely see about arranging a follow-up for you with your provider.
We'll mention this to the ER team, so they are aware of the equipment.
So far, nothing has presented a concern, and everything is moving smoothly.
We should be at the hospital shortly now.
Heart rhythm is sinus with premature atrial contractions-still normal and stable.
A gentle reminder to stay still through this upcoming turn.
Heart rate is stable again-68 beats per minute.
You are doing very well, Harold.
The hospital is right in sight now.
Please stay engaged and focused throughout the approach.
We will hand you over to the ER team shortly.
Prepare for a smooth offload from the ambulance to the ED table.
The transport is complete and all safe.
GCS is 15, indicating awake and fully oriented in all four parameters.
Peripheral pulses both in arms and legs are strong and regular. Skin is warm and dry.
EtCO2 waveform shows no evidence of bronchospasm, with consistent high 40s around the baseline.
All vitals remain stable and consistent with prior assessments during transport.
12-lead EKG was recorded before departure and again en route-no significant findings reported.
Both neck and head assessments show no scars, swelling, or masses.
We note your history of type 2 diabetes, primary hypertension, and COPD.
Based on these findings, we will relay a diagnosis of pulmonary edema with acute distress and no evidence of STEMI.
Breathing is still labored with physical strain and audible wheezing. Shortness of breath is partially managed by CPAP.
No new interventions needed during the transport phase.
All monitors, interventions, and restraints are in-place and confirmed during handover.
Harold is alert and oriented to person, place, time, and event-as noted in the ePCR-AAOx4.
